coredata.dc:title: Bismuth-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective fluorescent probe for metronidazole detection through inner filter effect, cytotoxicity studies, and bioimaging in Zebrafish
coredata.prism:publicationName: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry
coredata.dc:creator: Saranya Devi, M.
Daniel Thangadurai, T.
Nataraj, D.
Naveen Kumar, K.
coredata.dc:description: Herein we report that Walnut Shell-derived bismuth-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (Bi-CQDs) in rice morphology with an average size of 3–4 nm were used for the highly selective nanomolar (nM) detection of metronidazole (MNZ) through the inner filter effect (IFE). The excitation-dependent Bi-CQDs show high selectivity, sensitivity, and fluorescence detection on different pH (1 → 13) with a limit of detection (LoD) of 15.72 nM (R2 = 0.99092). The fluorescence quenching mechanism was evaluated by the interaction of Bi-CQSs with various MNZ concentrations (200 nM–2 μM), with binding constant as Ka = 1.081 × 10−3 M−1, increase in fluorescence lifetime values (5.60 → 5.88 ns). The interference studies, the effect of pH, the effect of time on MNZ detection, and the stability of the Bi-CQD•MNZ complex were also investigated. Further, in vitro bioimaging of Zebra fish and cytotoxicity studies were also conducted. Fluorescence imaging of Bi-CWDS in Zebra fishes distinguishes the morphology changes and embryogenesis. These results suggest that the developed sensor is highly biocompatible for fluorescence sensing and biological applications.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Bi-CQDs
Metronidazole
Rice morphology
IFE
Bioimaging

xocs:meta.xocs:srctitle: Journal of Photochemistry and Photobiology A: Chemistry

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: In vivo toxicological assessment in Danio rerio

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Authors MSD and TDT respectfully thank the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for research facilities, institutional research fellowship, and a seed grant. DN expresses appreciation for the UGC-CPEPA program at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore. Authors MSD and TDT acknowledge SAIF, MGU University, Kottayam for Raman analysis, and Dr. K. Kadirvelu, DRDO-Bharathiar University, Life Science Centre, Coimbatore, for his constant support.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) XRD, and (b) Raman spectrum of Bi-CQDs.
(A) FESEM analysis of Bi-CQDs (a) and (b), and (B) AFM analysis of Bi-CQDs in (a) 2D and (b) 3D modes.

HRTEM images of Bi-CQDs obtained at (a) 50 nm, (b) 20 nm, (c) 10 nm scale, (d) histogram of particle size distribution, (e) graphitic core lattice fringes from 2 nm scale, and (f) SAED pattern of Bi-CQDs.
(a) XPS survey spectrum of Bi-CQDs, (b) C1s, (c) N1s, (d) O1s, and (e) Bi4f.

(a) UV–visible absorption spectra of Bi-CQDs. {(inset) corresponding Bi-CQDs solution under (a) visible light and (b) UV light (λmax 350 nm)}, (b) PL emission spectra of Bi-CQDs at different excitation wavelengths (300 → 450 nm), (c) Fluorescence-quenching spectrum of Bi-CQDs upon adding various antibiotics drugs, (d) Fluorescence emission intensity changes of Bi-CQDs with increased concentration of MNZ (200 nM to 2 µM) (λex 350 nm; slit width 3 nm), and (e) Corresponding linear fit.

(a) The overlap between the excitation of Bi-CQDs at 350 nm and the emission spectrum of MNZ at 420 nm, and (b) Fluorescence decay profile of Bi-CQDs in the absence and presence of MNZ (0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 µM) (λex 450 nm).

(a) PL emission of Bi-CQDs in pH, 01–07, and (b) pH 07–13; (c) Bi-CQDs with MNZ (100 µM) at different time intervals (0 to 30 min), and (d) Corresponding fluorescence intensity bar diagram; (e) Competitive studies of Bi-CQDs•MNZ (10 µM) with other antibiotic drugs (SMZ, TET, and TMP), biomolcules (AA, UA, and DA), and metal ions (Na, Mg, Ca, and K) (100 µM), and (f) Corresponding fluorescence intensity bar diagram (λex 350 nm; slit width 3 nm).

Examination of the cytotoxic effect of Bi-CQDs (0, 5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µM) with MTT assay of L929 cell line. (a) Control cells without adding Bi-CQDs, (b) L929 cells + 5 µM Bi-CQDs, (c) L929 cells + 25 µM Bi-CQDs, (d) L929 cells + 50 µM Bi-CQDs, (e) L929 cells + 75 µM Bi-CQDs, and (f) L929 cells + 100 µM Bi-CQDs.

(a) Toxic effect of Bi-CQDs on embryogenesis of D. rerio at 24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf, and (b) bright-field image, fluorescence image, and merged image of a zebrafish incubated with Bi-CQDs. The images were captured at a magnification of ×4. Scale bar = 1,000 µm.
Relative fluorescence and average fluorescence lifetime of Bi-CQDs in the absence and presence of MNZ (0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 µM) (λex 450 nm).

head.ce:title: Bismuth-doped carbon quantum dots as an effective fluorescent probe for metronidazole detection through inner filter effect, cytotoxicity studies, and bioimaging in Zebrafish

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology, KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 641407, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology
KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, and Centre for Nanoscience and Technology, KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology, Coimbatore 641407, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Departments of Physics, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India
ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Departments of Physics, Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India

ce:affiliation.ce:label: c
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: BU-DRDO Center for Life Science Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: BU-DRDO Center for Life Science Bharathiar University

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: BU-DRDO Center for Life Science Bharathiar University, Coimbatore 641046, Tamilnadu, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Herein we report that Walnut Shell-derived bismuth-doped Carbon Quantum Dots (Bi-CQDs) in rice morphology with an average size of 3–4 nm were used for the highly selective nanomolar (nM) detection of metronidazole (MNZ) through the inner filter effect (IFE). The excitation-dependent Bi-CQDs show high selectivity, sensitivity, and fluorescence detection on different pH (1 → 13) with a limit of detection (LoD) of 15.72 nM (R2 = 0.99092). The fluorescence quenching mechanism was evaluated by the interaction of Bi-CQSs with various MNZ concentrations (200 nM–2 μM), with binding constant as Ka = 1.081 × 10−3 M−1, increase in fluorescence lifetime values (5.60 → 5.88 ns). The interference studies, the effect of pH, the effect of time on MNZ detection, and the stability of the Bi-CQD•MNZ complex were also investigated. Further, in vitro bioimaging of Zebra fish and cytotoxicity studies were also conducted. Fluorescence imaging of Bi-CWDS in Zebra fishes distinguishes the morphology changes and embryogenesis. These results suggest that the developed sensor is highly biocompatible for fluorescence sensing and biological applications.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: The data that has been used is confidential.

ce:section.ce:para: Metronidazole (MNZ, 1-(2-hydroxyethyl)-2-methyl-5-nitroimidazole), is a well-known nitroimidazole derivative antibiotic drug used in the treatment of bacterial infection, trichomonas, liver abscess, amoebic dysentery, and giardia, among others [1]. It can penetrate bacterial plasma membranes and hinder the replication of their DNA. However, extreme usage of MNZ causes adverse health effects, for instance, seizures, peripheral neuropathy, and ataxia [2]. Consequently, its usage in food and poultry processing industries is strictly prohibited to prevent contamination of meat products [3]. Therefore, it is essential to develop a highly sensitive and rapid method for MNZ determination.

Up to now, many analytical techniques, have been explored for the determination of MNZ including gas chromatography, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) [4], voltammetry [5], chemiluminescence [6], and capillary electrophoresis [7]. However, developing simple, highly sensitive, selective, and portable methods for the detection of MNZ is amenable to sustainable human health [8]. In addition to these techniques, fluorescence analysis has gained significant attention due to its cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, user-friendly nature, reliability, and low detection limits [9–13]. In addition to these techniques, fluorescence analysis has gained significant attention due to its cost-effectiveness, high sensitivity, user-friendly nature, reliability, and low detection limits.

In recent years, quantum dots (QDs) have emerged as the perfect material for advancing biosensors in the field of antibiotic detection. QDs represent a novel class of fluorescent nanomaterials, characterized by inorganic cores enveloped by organic molecules, all within the nanoscale range of 1–10 nm [14]. A new class of carbon nanomaterial, carbon quantum dots (CQDs), was synthesized by utilizing various carbon sources such as sucrose, chitosan, and citric acid. Importantly, these materials tend to overlook concerns regarding cost, toxicity, and environmental pollution [15]. In contrast, some researchers have successfully transformed low-cost biomass, often regarded as waste, into valuable CQDs [16]. This approach offers advantages such as renewability, reduced pollution, and widespread availability. In recent years, researchers have employed biomass sources such as plants and their derivatives, animal byproducts, and municipal waste for the production of biomass-derived CQDs [17]. CQDs are superior fluorescent sensor nanomaterials due to their distinctive photophysical properties, along with low toxicity, high chemical stability, and low environmental impact [18]. Furthermore, CQDs are new, small carbon nanoparticles that have special fluorescence features because they have surface functional groups like sp2 or sp3 hybridized carbon, oxygen, carboxyl, and hydroxyl bonding [19]. However, to further improve the fluorescence performance and broaden the scope of CQDs, two types of modification have been proposed, including surface functionalization and heteroatom doping [20].

The effective methods for enhancing the fluorescence properties and expanding the uses of heteroatom doping in CQDs. When nitrogen, sulfur, and other elements are introduced as dopants into CQDs, it is possible to enhance their spectral properties and expand their range of applications. The incorporation of nitrogen and sulfur elements brings about alterations in the electron density, structure, and composition of the doped CQDs, ultimately leading to an increase in the fluorescence intensity of both N-CQDs and S-CQDs [21,22]. This enhancement serves to elevate the overall performance of CQDs and broaden their potential applications. However, doping metallic ions into CQDs significantly tunes the physicochemical properties of CQDs. The presence of electrons and unoccupied orbitals on metal ions facilitates their interaction with amino and carboxyl groups on the CQD surface during chemical reactions [23]. Hence, the emergence of metal ions-doped CQDs (M-CQDs) has gained considerable attention as an alternative doping strategy. These M-CQDs, doped with various metal ions, not only exhibit exceptional optical properties but also introduce novel intrinsic characteristics through the ion doping process [24]. Doping CQDs with bismuth (Bi) can introduce unique material properties and functionalities [25]. Bi is an element naturally found on earth, belongs to the pentavalent post-transition metals group, and is commonly found in commercial ores in the form of oxides and sulfides. Bi possesses a distinctive set of properties, including low thermal conductivity, a high Hall coefficient, elevated electrical resistivity, and notable relevance in the field of medicine [26]. With a relatively low melting point of 271 °C, Bi is also known for its relative non-toxicity. Its versatile range of applications spans cosmetics, pigments, pharmaceuticals (constituting 63 % of its usage), metallurgy for processes like galvanizing and casting (26 %), alloys, solders, and ammunition (7 %), as well as research endeavors (4 %) [27]. Bi is considered less toxic than some other elements commonly used in QDs, such as cadmium [28]. This makes bismuth-doped CQDs a safer choice for biological and medical applications.

In this work, Bi-CQDs were synthesized through the hydrothermal method using Walnut biomass and used for selective fluoresce detection of MNZ. The fluorescence quenching mechanism of Bi-CQDs with MNZ interaction was evaluated by fluorescence and time-resolved lifetime decay methods. The Bi-CQDs exhibited high selectivity for MNZ detection, the pH effect, incubation time, and competitiveness in MNZ detection were also tested. Further, cytotoxicity of biomass-derived Bi-CQDS and in vitro bioimaging in the Zebrafish model were carried out.

Bi-CQDs were prepared by hydrothermal method (Scheme S1). A few grams of carbon material (carbonized walnut shell powder), 0.02 M Bismuth (III) nitrate pentahydrate (Bi(NO3)3·5H2O), and 40 mL of deionized (DI) water were mixed. The resulting mixture was stirred for 30 min at 40 °C, transferred to an autoclave, and kept for 4 h at 200 °C. After cooling to RT, the solution was centrifuged at 4000 rpm and finally filtered by 0.22 µm syringes. The resulting dark yellow solution containing Bi-CQDs was utilized for characterization and further studies.

Powder X-ray diffraction (XRD) was collected on Bruker D8 advance with the sources of Cu-Kα (1.56 Å) radiation. Surface morphology was analyzed on FESEM SIGMA HV-carl Zeiss with Bruker quanta 200-Z10 EDS’ detector at Coimbatore Institute of Technology, Coimbatore, India. Raman spectra were captured using a spectrometer of (Alpha 300RA) at SAIF, MGU, Kottayam, India. AFM images were recorded on the WiTec alpha 300, Germany, at PSGIAS, Coimbatore, India. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectra were recorded using a Shimadzu IR Affinity model 1 s at PSGIAS, Coimbatore, India. HRTEM was analyzed with Jeol/JEM 2100 at Cochin University, Kerala, India. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was analyzed by XPS, K-Alpha-KAN9954133 at Pondicherry University, Pondicherry, India. The absorption spectra of UV analysis were carried on a Shimadzu UV-1900 spectrometer. Fluorescence analysis was carried out with a Fluoromax4 spectrofluorometer at DRDO – Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. The IBH data station software V2.6 (Horiba – Deltaflax) was used to perform the time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements using the time-correlated single photon counting (TCSPC) technique at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India. Cytotoxicity was analyzed at the South India Textile Research Association (SITRA), Coimbatore, India. Bio-imaging studies were observed under an inverted microscope at 4x magnification (EVOS FLC, Life Technologies, USA) at DRDO- Bharathiar University, Coimbatore, India.

The Methyl Thiazolyl Tetrazolium (MTT) assay was employed to find out the cell viability of Bi-CQDs against L929 cells. Fetal bovine serum (FBS) was supplemented in a MEM medium for the incubation of L929 cells. The L929 cells were cultivated in MEM medium at 37 °C within a 5 % CO2 environment. After a 24-h incubation period, the cells underwent treatment with various concentrations of the Bi-CQDs solution (0.5, 2.5, 5, 7.5, and 10 µM). Phosphate Buffer Saline (PBS) with a pH of 7.2 was utilized for the thorough washing of the cells. Subsequently, MTT (1 mg/mL) was added to each cell well, and the cells were incubated for an additional 3–4 h. Following the completion of the incubation period, the culture medium was withdrawn. To dissolve the crystallized formazan, Dimethyl sulfoxide (DMSO; 100 %) was introduced. Inverted phase contrast microscopy was employed to detect the optical absorbance of each solution at 570 nm, enabling the estimation of cell viability using the following equation [29]:
(1)
Cell
viability =
OD e
OD c
× 100 %
where ODe is the absorbance of the experimental group, and ODc is the absorbance of the control group.

ce:section.ce:section-title: In vivo toxicological assessment in Danio rerio
ce:section.ce:para: D. rerio is chosen as an ideal vertebrate model organism due to its transparent embryos, allowing continuous monitoring of organogenesis [30]. Here, adult D. rerio was sourced from Coimbatore, Tamil Nadu, and acclimatized following OECD 2013 guidelines. Breeding involved a 2:1 male-to-female ratio, and eggs were collected for embryotoxicity testing per OECD 236 guidelines. The experiment included control and four Bi-CQDs treatment groups (10–40 µg/mL). Embryos (n = 50/group) were individually exposed in 5-well beakers from 24 to 96 hpf, with observations at 24 to 96 hpf using an inverted microscope. This revealed no significant changes in fish morphology and confirmed Bi-CQDs' excellent fluorescence properties as tags in various fish organs.

MNZ is widely used to treat certain infections of the stomach, liver, skin infection, lungs, bloodstream, etc. However, an overdose of MNZ causes significant side effects such as diarrhea, nausea, headache, vomiting, weight loss, and nervous system. Therefore, it is essential to detect even trace quantities of MNZ for human health care. Recently, carbon-based nanostructure materials have been a topic of research interest for the detection of MNZ using various techniques [31]. In this present work, simple and cost-effective fluorescence material (Bi-CQDs) has been utilized for the detection of MNZ through the inner filter effect (IFE). The CQDs were synthesized from walnut shell biomass waste. The as-prepared Bi-CQDs were characterized by standard physicochemical techniques and utilized for various application studies.

The XRD pattern of CQDs shows a broad diffraction peak around 2θ 21° to 29°, which may related to the plane (002) reflection of graphitic-like carbon [32]. However, the Bi-CQDs presented a broad peak around 2θ 25° to 34° along with additional peaks at 44°, and 67° were attributed to (110), (111), and (112) planes, respectively (Fig. 1 a). It is observed that the peak shift of characteristic peaks in Bi-CQDs is due to a certain incorporation of Bi element in CQDs. The broadening of such diffraction peaks might be related to the CQDs nanometer size [33–36]. The observation of the (110) plane in Bi-CQDs indicates the amorphous nature and the existence of a highly disordered graphite phase of CQDs. The XRD results suggest that the doping of Bi in CQDs enhances the surface defects in the CQDs.

Raman spectroscopy was performed to evaluate the crystal structure of CQDs and Bi-doped CQDs. In Fig. 1b, the Raman spectrum for the as-prepared Bi-CQDs shows two distinct bands at 1345 and 1566 cm−1, indicating the presence of the D and G bands, respectively. Comparing Raman spectra of previously reported CQDs, the D and G bands appear at 1340 and 1572 cm−1, along with C–H vibration at 1437 cm−1 [32]. During the doping of Bi with CQDs, the C–H band disappeared along with a red shift in the G band. The bands correspond to the sp3 defects and sp2 carbon, displaying the blue shift (D band) in the doped Bi-CQDs [37]. The ID/IG ratio has shown a slight increase of 0.86 compared to the pure CQDs (0.85) [32]. These Raman spectroscopic findings notably confirm the minimal presence of surface defects and a moderately graphitic nature in the Bi-CQDs structure [17].

To determine the morphology of Bi-CQDs, the FESEM images were recorded. The FESEM images reveal that Bi-CQDs are in a rice shape (Fig. 2 A), and the presence of carbon (51.18 %), nitrogen (7.14 %), oxygen (41.38 %), and Bismuth (0.31 %) are confirmed by EDS (Fig. S1a). The morphology of earlier reported as-prepared CQDs was found to be spherical with carbon (45.47 %), nitrogen (1.20 %), and oxygen (53.33 %) content [26]. This morphology change (spherical → rice) and EDS data of Bi-CQDs confirmed the successful doping of Bi element on the surface of the functional group. The AFM image related to topographic and morphologic records of the Bi-CQDs is shown in Fig. 2B. Largely, graphene can be classified into single, double, and multilayer structures based on its thickness. Thus, Bi-CQDs were found to have a thickness of 4.25 nm which is equivalent to more than 2–4 layers of graphene [38] and the height profile of the Bi-CQDs showed a nearly 30 nm. These AFM results revealed that roughness increases with the number of deposited layers in the graphite structure, as displayed in Fig. S1b.

The in-depth morphological profiles of Bi-CQDs and their corresponding Selected Area Electron Diffraction (SAED) patterns were obtained using HRTEM analysis (Fig. 3 a–f). The HRTEM morphology of Bi-CQDs displays irregularly shaped particles with observable agglomeration (Fig. 3a–c). The particle size histogram shows a higher magnification image of Bi-CQDs on the surface, with an average particle size of 3–4 nm (Fig. 3d). The obtained lattice fringes with a d-spacing of 0.24 nm corresponding to the (110) plane of graphene and the absence of the (002) plane of graphite indicates that the Bi-CQDs were doped a few layers of graphene sheets (Fig. 3e) [39]. The diffracted rings in the SAED pattern suggest that Bi-CQDs are not crystals but have an amorphous structure. These results were in good agreement with the XRD results and confirmed the successful doping of Bi in CQDs (Fig. 3f).

The chemical composition and chemical bonds of Bi-CQDs were further investigated by the XPS technique. The XPS survey scans of Bi-CQDs show three peaks at 285.41, 400.95, 532.96, and 153 eV, which correspond to C1s, N1s, O1s, and Bi4f, with atomic percentages of 72.79 %, 1.57 %, and 25.64 %, respectively (Fig. 4 a). The high-resolution spectrum of C1s exhibits three peaks at 284.8, 286.1, and 288.3 eV, indicating the presence of sp2 carbon (CC/C–C), sp3 carbon (C–O/C–N/C–Bi), and CO group (Fig. 4b) [40]. The XPS spectra of N1s reveal three dominant peaks at 400.0, 401.7, and 406.7 eV, indicating the presence of C–N, CN, and NO bonds in Bi-CQDs (Fig. 4c). Deconvolution of the O1s peaks yields two components: CO and O–CO that are observed at 531.6 and 532.8 eV, respectively (Fig. 4d). These observations point out the distinct chemical bonds, with the former attributed to lattice oxygen and the latter associated with surface-adsorbed oxygen [41]. Furthermore, in the Bi4f high-resolution XPS spectra of Bi-CQDS, the spin–orbit doublet peaks at 168 eV(Bi 4f5/2) and 153 eV with a peak separation of 15 eV are related to Bi 4f5/2 and Bi 4f7/2, respectively (Fig. 4e). It is observed that most of the Bi ions in Bi-CQDs are in the Bi3+ oxidation state [42]. After deconvolute the Bi4f high-resolution spectra of Bi-CQDs, the doublet peaks reveal one smaller peak and another peak containing a sub-peak. Notably, the peak corresponding to the Bi+3 state exhibits higher intensity and a larger area under the curve compared to the peak corresponding to the Bi0 state. This discrepancy confirms the major presence of the Bi2O3 phase on the surface and a partially metallic Bi0 phase in the sample [43]. As a result, the XPS analysis confirms the formation of Bi-CQDs with a charge transfer process between Bi and CQDs.

The FT-IR spectra of Bi-CQDs display several peaks corresponding to different functional groups (Fig. S3). The hydroxyl functional group (υOH) peak is observed at 3790 cm−1. Less intense and sharp peaks are perceived at 2628 and 2293 cm−1, which are attributed to the sp3 C–H bond vibration and NCO stretching bond, respectively. Furthermore, the peaks at 1350, 1213, and 904 cm−1, correspond to the hydroxyl group (υOH), stretching (υC–O), and bending (υCC), respectively [44]. The other peaks observed at 640 and 559 cm−1 are due to υBi–O of Bi-CQDs [45]. These stretching and bending bands reveal the presence of various functional groups, such as hydroxyl, carboxyl, and bismuth oxide, in Bi-CQDs, which is supported by the XPS results.

In the UV–vis absorption spectrum of Bi-CQDs, one broad absorption band was observed at 280 nm correlated to the typical π → π* transitions of CC in Bi-CQDs (Fig. 5 a). The absorption spectrum of undoped CQDs was recorded. The bare CQDs exhibited an absorption peak centered at 278 nm corresponding to the π → π* transition associated with the presence of sp2 hybridized carbon [46]. These results are in agreement with our previous report on CQDs [32]. However, the absorption spectrum of Bi-CQDs has shown a slight red-shifted (∼2 nm) absorption spectrum at 280 nm compared with undoped CQDs. The dopant Bi alters the energy level of the π → π* transition (associated with the sp2 hybridized carbon core), thereby modifying the electronic structure, and consequently, the optical properties of CQDs [47]. The Bi-CQDs solution exhibits a strong blue fluorescence under the illumination of UV light and pale yellow under visible light as shown in the inset of Fig. 5a. While doping the metal (Bi) in CQDs, the preodement absorbance peak intensity has been decreased slightly.

The fluorescence spectra of the Bi-CQDs were measured at different excitation wavelengths as shown in Fig. 5b. The fluorescence spectra of Bi-CQDs show excitation-dependent emission properties when the Bi-CQDs were excited with different excitation wavelengths ranging from 300 to 450 nm (Fig. 5b). It is found that the intensity of the fluorescence emission peak increases with an increase in excitation wavelength from 300 to 350 nm, and the maximum fluorescence excitation wavelength was obtained at 350 nm. On the other hand, as the excitation wavelength was increased further from 360 to 450 nm, there was a gradual decrease in the emission intensity. It's important to underline that the fluorescence emission peaks exhibit a redshift as the excitation wavelength increases, signifying the presence of photon reabsorption which underscores the excitation-dependent nature of the fluorescence property of Bi-CQDs [1]. This excitation-dependent fluorescence property reflected the size of the Bi-CQDs and the distribution of different surface states [48].

Comparative experiments were conducted to assess the selectivity and sensitivity of Bi-CQDs towards MNZ drugs which include other antibiotic drugs such as Sulfamethoxazole (SMZ), Tetracycline (TET), and Trimethoprim (TMP) (Fig. 5c). The fluorescence emission intensity of Bi-CQDs (10 mM) was decreased upon the addition of all the tested antibiotics drugs such as SMZ, TET, TMP, and MNZ (100 μM). Notably, the fluorescence emission intensity of Bi-CQDs was quenched more only upon the addition of MNZ. The addition of other antibiotic drugs into Bi-CQDs slightly changes its fluorescence emission intensity, when compared to the addition of MNZ. It confirms the selectivity of Bi-CQD towards MNZ in physiological conditions.

Based on the selectivity results, the sensitivity of Bi-CQDs towards MNZ was investigated by introducing various concentrations of MNZ (200 nM → 2 μM) into an aqueous solution of Bi-CQDs (10 mM) (Fig. 5d). The gradual decrease in fluorescence intensity of Bi-CQDs at 440 nm (λ ex 350 nm) with increasing concentrations of MNZ could be attributed to the inner filter effect (IFE), as illustrated in Fig. 6 a. This effect is due to arise from the formation of a ground-state complex, Bi-CQDs•MNZ, upon the interaction between Bi-CQDs and MNZ [49]. This formation of the ground-state complex explains the non-radiative recombination of electrons and holes by effectively transferring photoelectrons or energy, consequently leading to further fluorescence quenching [50]. Fig. 6a illustrates this non-radiative energy transfer process, which occurs when the excitation of Bi-CQDs overlaps with the emission of MNZ. Additionally, this overlap between the emission of Bi-CQDs and the excitation of MNZ is well supported by the absorption spectrum in Fig. S4a and the normalized spectrum in Fig. S4b. Here, Bi-CQDs act as a donor, and MNZ acts as an acceptor which enables the energy transfer for the formation of the ground-state complex. Fig. S4c displays the UV absorption spectra of Bi-CQDs, MNZ, and the mixed solution of Bi-CQDs + MNZ. It reveals that Bi-CQDs exhibit an absorption peak at 280 nm, MNZ at 320 and 230 nm, and the Bi-CQDs + MNZ at 281 and 320 nm. The obtained data indicates that peaks that appeared in the UV absorption spectrum of Bi-CQDs + MNZ are the sum of the absorbance of the two components such as Bi-CQDs and MNZ. Notably, there is no other peak was observed due to the formation of any by-product. Therefore, it is confirmed that there is no formation of new substances or products when Bi-CQDs interact with MNZ. These results corroborate that the inner filter effect (IFE) does not generate new substances or products by the interaction between Bi-CQDs and MNZ, as evidenced by the obtained UV absorption spectrum (Fig. S4c). The calibration equation for this relationship is expressed as F/F0, with an associated coefficient (R2) value of 0.99092 (Fig. 5e) and a minimum detectable concentration of 15.72 nM (Fig. S4d). In this context, F and F0 represent the fluorescence emission intensity of Bi-CQDs when MNZ is absent and present, respectively. The obtained association constant (Ka = 1.081 × 10−3 M−1) with less limit of detection (LOD) value (15.72 nM) enhances the practical application of as-prepared Bi-CQDs in the biological field for sensing MNZ in physiological conditions.

A time-correlated single-photon counting experiment was carried out to observe the fluorescence lifetime profile of Bi-CQDs in the presence and absence of MNZ (Fig. 6b). In the absence of MNZ, the fluorescence lifetime of Bi-CQDs is best fitted with a tri-exponential function with an average lifetime of 5.60 ns (Table 1 ). This tri-exponential function fitting (Eq. (3)) explicates that fluorescence decay occurs through three relaxation pathways.
(3)
F
t
=
A 1
exp
- t /
τ 1
+
A 2
exp
- t /
τ 2
+
A 3
exp
- t /
τ 3
where “τ” is the fluorescence lifetime and “A” represents the amplitude of the corresponding lifetime. Upon addition of a different concentration of MNZ (0.25, 0.50, and 1.0 eq.), the decay profile curve of Bi-CQDs showed a slight increase in average lifetime value (5.60 → 5.88 ns) without changing its tri-exponential functionality. This may be due to IFE, under these conditions the fluorescence lifetime of the Bi-CQDs remains constant whether the analyte, MNZ, is present or absent. This indicates the absence of complexation in the excited state. Particularly, the peak of the Bi-CQDs remains unchanged in the presence of the MNZ, affirming that no new complexes are formed in the ground state as well (Fig. 6b) [51].

Fluorescence emission intensity changes of Bi-CQDs upon binding with MNZ were examined in different pH (1 → 13). Under 365 nm excitation, the fluorescence intensity of Bi-CQD•MNZ was found to be rapidly increased in an acidic environment (pH 7 → 1) and enhanced emission was caused by the high recombination process. However, at pH 7, the Bi-CQD•MNZ complex is highly stable and the maximum fluorescence was obtained. Conversely, the fluorescence intensity in basic conditions was decreased (pH 7 → 14), due to the protonation –COOH functional groups (Fig. 7 a). These results suggest that Bi-CQDs possess emission characteristics through the interaction of MNZ at various pH and susceptible material for different biological and sensor applications [52].

Bi-CQDs undergo an immediate fluorescence quenching at 440 nm upon the addition of MNZ (100 µM) and require less than 1 min to attain > 95 % of maximum fluorescence emission intensity. After one minute, it was observed that the initially attained fluorescence emission intensity was not changed even when the reaction time increased up to 30 min (Fig. 7c). This suggests that sensing of MNZ by Bi-CQDs is stable and extremely fast (Fig. 7d). To confirm the strong binding of Bi-CQD with MNZ, interference studies were performed with other antibiotics (SMZ, TET, and TMP), biomolecules (Ascorbic acid (AA), Uric acid (UA), and Dopamine (DA)), and naturally occurring metal ions (Na, Mg, Ca, and K) in the presence of Bi-CQD•MNZ (10 µM). The addition of a 10-fold higher concentration (100 µM) of other antibiotic drugs, biomolecules, and metal ions, didn’t impact the fluorescence quenching caused by Bi-CQDs•MNZ (Fig. 7e and f) [43]. The decrease in the fluorescence emission intensity of the Bi-CQD•MNZ complex upon the addition of the aforementioned interferons might be attributed to the dilution of the corresponding complex solutions. This phenomenon reveals that none of the aforementioned interferons affect the strong binding nature of the Bi-CQD•MNZ complex, even at a 10-fold higher concentration, and they do not compete with MNZ as well. Overall, these results further demonstrated that as-prepared Bi-CQDs show high selectivity towards MNZ.

Assessing the cytotoxicity or biocompatibility of Bi-CQDs in cell lines holds great significance for its biomedical applications [29]. To investigate the interaction between cells and Bi-CQDs, L929 cells were employed in an MTT assay experiment to assess the cell viability of Bi-CQDs. The cell viability analysis was conducted while the cells were cultured in various concentrations of the Bi-CQDs solution (5, 25, 50, 75, and 100 µM) for 24 h (Fig. 8 a–f). Following a 24-h incubation period, the corresponding cytotoxicity values were observed as 77, 69, 53, 42, and 36 % for the respective concentrations of Bi-CQDs. Additionally, Fig. S5b illustrates a mild to moderate cytotoxicity (36 %) in the high concentration of Bi-CQDs (100 µM) after a 24-h incubation period with L929 cells. Furthermore, the calculated IC50 value was determined to be 1.76 µM (as shown in Fig. S5a), based on the time of contact assay [53]. Based on this result, the cytotoxicity effect of Bi-CQDs is moderate at higher concentrations against L929 cell lines which suggests that Bi-CQDs can be a good candidate for biomedical applications.

In the present study, the toxicity effects of Bi-CQDs were carried out using Zebra fish at various doses of Bi-CQDs (10, 20, 30, and 40 µg/mL). The in vivo toxicity was evaluated by measuring the changes in morphology, mortality, and embryogenesis at various time intervals of 24, 48, 72, and 96 hpf as shown in Fig. 9 a [54]. After 24 hpf, embryogenesis growth was normal at doses 10–40 µg/mL, and pericardial edema was observed at treatment of 40 µg/mL of Bi-CQDs. Nevertheless, the mortality rate was not found at 10–40 µg/mL dose ranges of Bi-CQDs (Fig. 9a). Numerous small black dots with blue fluorescence are distributed throughout the fish, particularly within certain organs and tissues. Nevertheless, they often remain inconspicuous due to the presence of larger black dots from the fish embryo itself. Importantly, the morphology of the fish embryo remains unaltered following incubation with Bi-CQDs at different hpf (24–96 hpf), signifying that these solutions do not cause harm to the fish embryo up to 96 hpf. As depicted in Fig. 9b, a substantial blue fluorescence emanates from the fish embryo that underwent Bi-CQDs incubation, highlighting the excellent fluorescence emission characteristics of Bi-CQDs, even after intake by the fish embryo. The merged images in Fig. 9b provide further confirmation that the abdomen, brain, and dorsum of the fish have assimilated Bi-CQDs, underscoring their potential utility as effective fluorescence tags due to their impressive blue fluorescence properties. In this present study, Bi-CQDs were shown to have a non-toxic effect in the fish embryo at a high concentration level of 40 µg/mL which enhances its practical applications.

In summary, blue-fluorescence Bi-CQDs were synthesized in an average size of 3–4 nm by the hydrothermal method. The XRD data revealed that the synthesized Bi-CQDs possess an amorphous graphitic structure, and microscopic images (FESEM and HRTEM) illustrate the rice morphology of Bi-CQDs. The presence of surface defects in Bi-CQDs was confirmed through Raman analysis. The doping of Bi and the existence of surface functional groups such as carbonyl and hydroxyl in Bi-CQDs was strongly confirmed by XPS. Furthermore, fluorescence studies confirmed that Bi-CQDs exhibit excitation dependence within the range of 300–450 nm. Additionally, the selective and highly sensitive MNZ sensing property of Bi-CQDs was corroborated by IFE through the formation of a ground state complex. The obtained LoD (15.72 nM) (R2 = 0.99092). suggests that Bi-CQDs can quantitatively detect MNZ antibiotics within a low concentration range (200 nM–2 μM). Moreover, evidence of IFE between Bi-CQDs and MNZ is provided by the tri-exponential fluorescence lifetime (5.60–5.88 ns) in both the absence and presence of MNZ. The slight to moderate cytotoxic reactivity in a concentration range of 5–100 μM with an IC50 value of 1.76 μM against the MTT assay L929 cell line, biocompatibility with cancer cells, and impact on very low toxic levels in vivo Zebrafish of Bi-CQDs elucidate its practicability. Overall, the as-prepared Bi-CQDs can strongly be considered as a suitable fluorescent sensor for MNZ and bioimaging applications.

M. Saranya Devi: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Formal analysis, Writing – original draft. T. Daniel Thangadurai: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Validation, Investigation, Data curation. D. Nataraj: Formal analysis, Validation. K. Naveen Kumar: Formal analysis, Validation.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: Authors MSD and TDT respectfully thank the KPR Institute of Engineering and Technology for research facilities, institutional research fellowship, and a seed grant. DN expresses appreciation for the UGC-CPEPA program at Bharathiar University, Coimbatore. Authors MSD and TDT acknowledge SAIF, MGU University, Kottayam for Raman analysis, and Dr. K. Kadirvelu, DRDO-Bharathiar University, Life Science Centre, Coimbatore, for his constant support.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.jphotochem.2023.115429.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
